channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: MoMath Brings Prime Numbers to a Prime New Location
item.description: After outgrowing its original home, the National Museum of Mathematics has added new exhibits and an art gallery space in what was an empty storefront along the Avenue of the Americas in Manhattan.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang and Emon Hassan

item.title: Lemurs Love This Fruit That Is Choking Madagascar’s Forests
item.description: The strawberry guava, one of the world’s worst invasive species, hinders forest restoration on the island while feeding its famous endangered primates.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Nuwer

item.title: NASA’s Asteroid-Smashing DART Mission Sped Up Space Rock’s Orbit Around the Sun
item.description: New data about the DART spacecraft’s effects adds evidence that Earth could be defended from future deadly asteroids by diverting their orbits.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: NASA/Johns Hopkins APL
item.media:description: Dimorphos is about 525 feet around and orbits a larger parent asteroid, Didymos.

item.title: You’ve Never Seen Ants Like This Before
item.description: The Antscan database captures the tiny insects’ diversity and shows how the world’s living things could be rendered in three dimensions.
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: Tree Rings Reveal Origins of Some of the World’s Best Violins
item.description: A study of over 275 violins suggests that Antonio Stradivari used wood from high-altitude forests in northern Italy to craft some of his most renowned instruments.
item.dc:creator: Katherine Kornei

item.title: Chimpanzees Are Really Into Crystals
item.description: In an attempt to understand our own fascination with the shiny minerals, researchers gave some to chimps.
item.dc:creator: Cara Giaimo

item.title: From Endangered Plants Come Whiffs of Inspiration
item.description: How scientists, conservationists and the fragrance industry are working to revive extinct scents and perpetuate threatened ones.
item.dc:creator: Tammy LaGorce

item.title: Superfood Fuels Mating Frenzy for Critically Endangered Kakapo
item.description: Kakapos, which are reclusive and flightless and can live as long as humans, are found only in New Zealand. They feed on the fruit of the rimu tree.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Andrew Digby/Dept. of Conservation, New Zealand, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A kakapo with eggs on Anchor Island, in New Zealand. The island is known for lacking the kinds of predators that threaten the parrots.

item.title: Experiment Shows Possibility of Martian Microbes Hitching a Ride to Earth
item.description: Hardy bacteria in a lab survived pressures comparable to an asteroid strike on the red planet, suggesting a hypothetical scenario in which our planet was seeded with life.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews

item.title: For These Design Materials, It’s Goodbye and Good Riddance
item.description: Not all acts of extinction are to be regretted.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Treffinger
item.media:credit: Armando Babani/European Pressphoto Agency
item.media:description: PFAS, so-called forever chemicals, were developed in the early 20th century, and are the reason Teflon cookware doesn’t stick, why many pizza boxes resist grease and why clothing can be waterproof.

item.title: Divisive F.D.A. Vaccine Regulator Is Resigning
item.description: Dr. Vinay Prasad drew criticism for overriding career scientists and rejecting drugs by companies seeking agency approval.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: U.S. Food and Drug Administration
item.media:description: Dr. Vinay Prasad’s refusal to accept Moderna’s application for a new mRNA flu vaccine, prompting an uproar among companies and experts.

item.title: From 1983: Lise Meitner, Physicist, Is Dead at 89; Paved Way for Splitting of Atom
item.description: She laid much of the theoretical groundwork for the atomic bomb, although she did not participate directly in its production.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Lise Meitner in an undated photo with the German nuclear chemist Otto Hahn.

item.title: From 2000: Hedy Lamarr, Sultry Star Who Reigned in Hollywood
item.description: A temptress on the silver screen in the 1930s and ’40s, she later became an inventor.
item.dc:creator: Richard Severo
item.media:credit: Clarence Sinclair Bull/Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Hedy Lamarr posing for a promotional portrait for the 1944 film “The Heavenly Body.”

item.title: Fewer Pregnant Women Received Acetaminophen in E.R.s After White House Warnings
item.description: A new analysis of prescription data found that emergency room orders for acetaminophen for pregnant women fell for weeks after the federal warning.
item.dc:creator: Azeen Ghorayshi
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump made an announcement about autism and Tylenol alongside Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. in the Roosevelt Room of the White House in Washington last September.

item.title: Feeling the Effects of 260,000 Federal Jobs Lost
item.description: One year in, assessing budget cuts to federal climate and science jobs.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:credit: Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images
item.media:description: Demonstrators gathered to protest against budget cuts outside the headquarters of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration in Silver Spring, Maryland, last year.

item.title: Nature Report, Killed by Trump, Is Released Independently
item.description: A draft assessment of the health of nature in the United States is grim but shot through with bright spots and possibility.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.category: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine
item.media:credit: David Robert Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: A dried part of the Arkansas River in Garden City, Kan.

item.title: Should New York City Burn Its Parks? This Scientist Thinks So.
item.description: An unusual outbreak of wildfires in city parks gave scientists a chance to study these rare events. Now they’re coming to different conclusions.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Karsten Moran
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times

item.title: F.D.A. Faces Upset Over Denials of New Drugs
item.description: Agency officials promise fast reviews of new treatments while vowing they will not be a “rubber stamp” for the industry. But patients with rare diseases view recent decisions as signs that the doors are closing on their options.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times

item.title: Trump’s High-Profile Oil and Gas Lease Sale in Alaska Has No Takers
item.description: No companies bid for the chance to drill in more than 1 million acres of water off Alaska’s Cook Inlet, the Interior Department said Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: Bureau of Ocean Energy Management, Regulation and Enforcement
item.media:credit: Nathaniel Wilder for The New York Times
item.media:description: An oil platform in the Cook Inlet last year.

item.title: Most Patients Keep Weight Off With Fewer GLP-1 Shots, Study Finds
item.description: In a recent review, a small group of patients received the injections less frequently but still maintained weight loss and health benefits.
item.dc:creator: Roni Caryn Rabin
item.media:credit: Kristian Thacker for The New York Times

item.title: Why Data Center Firms Are Working With Trump on Energy Costs
item.description: The White House has floated a new plan to try to ease voter concerns over the A.I. boom’s effect on electric bills. But it won’t be easy to put into practice.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Christie Hemm Klok for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Google data center. Large data centers can use as much electricity as a small city.

item.title: TerraPower Nuclear Reactor in Wyoming Gets Federal Permit
item.description: TerraPower’s project in Wyoming, which uses novel technology, is the first new commercial reactor to receive federal approval in nearly a decade.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Benjamin Rasmussen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Politicians and executives, including Bill Gates, shoveled dirt during the groundbreaking for TerraPower’s Natrium nuclear power plant in Kemmerer, Wyo.

item.title: Sea Levels Are Already Higher Than Many Scientists Think, New Study Shows
item.description: Researchers found that a majority of studies on coastal sea levels underestimated how high water levels are, and hundreds of millions of people are closer to peril than previously thought.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Mira Rojanasakul
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Juan Barreto/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A stretch of coast in Suriname, one of many places affected by sea level rise and erosion caused by climate change

item.title: Kristi Noem Review Delayed FEMA Aid by Weeks, Senate Finds
item.description: A report by Senate Democrats found that scrutiny of large FEMA expenditures slowed housing and employment assistance for victims of fires and storms.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Al Drago/Getty Images
item.media:description: Kristi Noem, the homeland security secretary, at the Federal Emergency Management Agency headquarters in Washington in January.

item.title: A Critical Nuclear Site in Iran Is Spared During U.S.-Israeli Airstrikes, for Now
item.description: The Isfahan facility, suspected of storing a cache of enriched uranium, was smashed during the 12-day campaign last June.
item.dc:creator: William J. Broad
item.media:credit: Raheb Homavandi/Reuters
item.media:description: A uranium processing plant in Isfahan, which hosts one of Iran’s major nuclear sites, seen in 2005 during its early stages of development. Experts suspect that mountains near the site, like those to the right, now harbor a complex of tunnels where Iran keeps a batch of uranium sufficient to make nine or 10 atomic bombs.

item.title: Potomac River Reopens for Some Uses After Sewer Collapse
item.description: Contamination levels in the waterway have declined, but health advisories remain in place for boaters downstream.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images
item.media:description: A volunteer looks at a section of the Potomac River before collecting a sample of water to test for E.coli on Feb. 19, 2026, in Cabin John, Md.

item.title: A Danish Program Takes On the Stigma of Mental Illness
item.description: One of Us, run by Denmark’s health ministry, works with people with mental health conditions to share their stories in schools, hospitals and police stations, helping turn fear into understanding.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj
item.media:credit: Mathias Eis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pernille Petersen and Giuseppe Parlatore, ambassadors for the program.

item.title: Your Closest Forest Gets Lit Up by a Hidden Purple Glow
item.description: Research shows that a phenomenon called St. Elmo’s Fire, which occurs during thunderstorms, may be much more common than previously understood.
item.dc:creator: Annie Roth

item.title: As Kennedy Turns From Vaccines, MAHA Allies See a ‘Betrayal’
item.description: As the midterm elections approach, the health secretary is focusing on “real food.” Anger is rising among some of his supporters.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mueller
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Callaghan O'Hare for The New York Times
item.media:description: Derrick Wynne was discharged from the Army for refusing a Covid shot. “He was using our movement to gain a huge amount of traction,” he said of Robert F. Kennedy, Jr.

item.title: Scientists Decry ‘Political Attack’ on the Reference Manual on Scientific Evidence
item.description: More than two dozen contributors to the manual criticized the deletion of a chapter on climate science by the Federal Judicial Center.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.category: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine
item.media:credit: Andrew Kelly/Reuters
item.media:description: The Thurgood Marshall Federal Judiciary Building where the Federal Judicial Center is located.

item.title: Photos: Blood Moon Total Lunar Eclipse 2026
item.description: Earth’s shadow temporarily draped the moon in a copper hue before sunrise on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:credit: Victor Medina/Reuters
